people.name: Hermann August Seger
people.notes: Established the foundational UMF system that underpins rational glaze calculation; contribution considered irreplaceable and enduring for 150+ years

people.name: Daniel Rhodes
known_for: Clay and Glazes for the Potter
people.notes: Standard text for generations; described as having a voice — unhurried, curious, authoritative. Multiple generations learned to think about materials from this book.

people.name: Bernard Leach
people.notes: Usually discussed aesthetically but contains serious chemistry; proto-terroir glaze work before the concept existed. Book treasured in a devotional way.

people.name: Frank Hamer
known_for: The Potter's Dictionary of Materials and Techniques
people.notes: Often underappreciated relative to actual influence. Encyclopedic reference, treasured like a trusted tool.

people.name: Janet Hamer
known_for: The Potter's Dictionary of Materials and Techniques
people.notes: Co-authored The Potter's Dictionary with Frank Hamer; the collaboration produced one of the most enduring reference works in ceramics.

people.name: John Britt
known_for: The Complete Guide to High-Fire Glazes
The Complete Guide to Mid-Range Glazes
people.notes: Most-used studio references of last two decades. Studio copies often have glaze drips on pages.

people.name: Linda Bloomfield
people.notes: Particularly strong on colorant chemistry; clean modern treatment bridging scientific explanation and studio practice.

people.name: Val Cushing
people.notes: Handbook circulated through Alfred's orbit, not commercially published. Treasured almost as artifact; having a copy feels like connection to a lineage.

people.name: Maija Grotell
people.notes: Known as the 'Mother of American Ceramics'; revolutionary educator focused on glaze chemistry.

people.name: Michael Cardew
people.notes: Known as a 'glaze wizard' in studio pottery.

people.name: Robin Hopper
people.notes: One of the most systematic treatments of color in glazes, organized around the periodic table of colorants. People describe it as the book that made color chemistry finally make sense.

people.name: Tony Hansen
people.notes: Arguably done more than anyone in the modern era to make glaze chemistry computationally accessible. Managed to be both accessible and technically serious at scale. Paradigm shift in the field.

people.name: John Hesselberth
people.notes: Co-authored the cone 6 reference that validated middle-temperature functional ware as worthy of rigorous chemistry attention.

people.name: Ian Currie
people.notes: Developed a genuinely elegant contribution to empirical glaze research. Described as the book that changed how potters think rather than just what they know.

people.name: Robert Tichane
people.notes: Self-published, small print runs, out of print. Treasured partly because hard to find. Obsessive writing quality comes through on every page. Influenced studio reduction firing practice in the West.

people.name: Richard Zakin
people.notes: Addressed oxidation firing chemistry seriously at a time when most literature assumed reduction.

people.name: Herbert Sanders
known_for: bridging Eastern and Western glaze traditions
people.notes: Bridged Eastern glaze traditions with Western scientific analysis at a time when that crossover was rare.

people.name: Nigel Wood
people.notes: Most rigorous scientific treatment of classical Chinese ceramic chemistry in English. Treasured by a smaller audience but with unusual intensity.

people.name: W.G. Lawrence
people.notes: Brought industrial ceramic engineering methodology into studio practice. More technically precise than most studio-oriented texts.

people.name: F.H. Norton
people.notes: Rigorous engineering texts that influenced generations of ceramic scientists and indirectly shaped studio practice through Alfred and Ohio State programs.

people.name: C.W. Parmelee
people.notes: Long considered the most comprehensive technical reference on glaze science from an industrial standpoint. Dense but irreplaceable. Out of print and sought after.

people.name: Edward Orton Jr.
people.notes: Cone system so embedded in practice that people forget it was a significant scientific contribution.
people.institution: Ohio State University / Orton Ceramic Foundation

people.name: Emmanuel Cooper
known_for: The Potter's Book of Glaze Recipes
people.notes: Bridged chemistry, history, and practice across multiple books. Long-running editor of Ceramic Review, shaping how the field communicated about technical matters for decades.
people.institution: Ceramic Review / City of Westminster College

people.name: Paul Soldner
people.notes: Transformed Western raku practice and forced rethinking of low-fire reduction chemistry. Post-firing reduction is chemically distinct from kiln reduction and was poorly understood before his work.

people.name: Warren MacKenzie
people.notes: Contributed substantially to understanding wood and reduction glaze behavior through decades of systematic practice and documentation.

people.name: Jack Troy
people.notes: Documented wood-fire and salt-fire chemistry with more analytical rigor than most atmospheric firing literature.

people.name: Byron Temple
known_for: sodium vapor interaction with clay bodies
people.notes: Did serious practical research on salt and soda glaze chemistry at high temperatures.

people.name: Shoji Hamada
people.notes: Left almost no written technical documentation by design, but posthumous analytical work on his glazes is chemically revealing — particularly his use of local Mashiko materials.

people.name: Coll Minogue
people.notes: Co-authored key wood-fire chemistry texts with Robert Sanderson; practical and analytical approach to ash and atmospheric glazes.

people.name: Robert Sanderson
people.notes: Co-authored wood-fire chemistry texts with Coll Minogue; systematic documentation of ash glaze behavior.

people.name: John Neely
people.notes: Brought spectroscopic methods to questions that previously relied on visual assessment.

people.name: Jeff Zamek
known_for: The Potter's Studio Clay and Glaze Handbook
people.notes: More recent practical reference covering materials and glaze formulation together.

people.name: Tony Birks
people.notes: Reached a mass audience and introduced glaze chemistry to potters who might not have sought it out.

people.name: Robert Desjardins
people.notes: Helped build the computational infrastructure underlying Digitalfire.

people.name: R.T. Stull
known_for: Stull Chart — SiO₂ vs. Al₂O₃ map predicting glaze surface quality (1912)
Systematic classification of glaze regions (matte, semi-matte, bright gloss, underfired, crazed)
Deformation temperature mapping by firing cone (1914 with Howat)
Establishing UMF-based glaze navigation still standard 110+ years later
people.notes: Published two landmark papers in the Transactions of the American Ceramic Society: Vol. XIV (1912) introducing the Stull Chart at cone 11, and Vol. XVI (1914) with W.L. Howat extending the work to cone 9. Both papers are public domain and indexed on Archive.org. His 1912 chart remains the foundational coordinate system for glaze formulation — the basis for Stull Atlas.

people.name: Derek Au (Philipau)
known_for: Glazy.org — largest open glaze chemistry database
people.notes: Created and maintains Glazy.org, the world's largest open ceramics database with 340K+ entries. Made UMF calculation and glaze chemistry accessible to every potter with a browser. Open-source (Laravel+Vue). The dataset that feeds Stull Atlas.

people.name: Charles Fergus Binns
people.institution: Alfred University / New York State College of Ceramics
people.notes: Son of the director of Royal Worcester. Founded the ceramics program at Alfred University in 1900 — the root node of American glaze education. Rhodes, Cushing, and almost everyone downstream traces back to Alfred. Wrote The Potter's Craft (1910).

people.name: Adelaide Alsop Robineau
people.notes: Editor of Keramic Studio (precursor to Ceramics Monthly). Crystalline glaze pioneer who achieved results nobody could replicate for decades. The Scarab Vase took 1000+ hours of carving on a single porcelain form. Applied scientific method to glaze development before it was standard practice.

people.name: Ron Roy
people.notes: Co-developer of the cone 6 oxidation movement. His systematic testing made cone 6 viable as a production temperature — directly influencing thousands of studios to move away from cone 10. Third leg of the Hansen-Hesselberth-Roy stool. Deeply concerned with glaze safety and leaching.

people.name: Taxile Doat
people.notes: Master ceramist at Sèvres who figured out zinc crystal growth before anyone had the phase diagrams to explain why. Grand Feu Ceramics (1905) was the first serious treatment of high-fire glaze chemistry from a practitioner. Directly influenced Robineau and the University City experiment.

people.name: W. David Kingery
people.notes: THE textbook author for ceramic material science. Introduction to Ceramics (1960, co-authored) defined the field for 60+ years. His thermal expansion, coefficient of thermal expansion, and glaze fit frameworks underpin half the calculations in modern glaze software. Bridged pure material science and applied ceramics.

people.name: Pete Pinnell
people.notes: One of the most respected living glaze chemistry educators. His workshops are deeply analytical — real chemistry, not just recipes. Systematic approach to crystalline, specialty, and surface-effect glazes. Teaches potters to think in oxides, not ingredients.

people.name: David Green
known_for: clear explanations of glaze chemistry for studio potters
people.notes: Wrote one of the clearest explanations of glaze chemistry for studio potters ever published. Understanding Pottery Glazes bridges the gap between industrial ceramic science and studio practice without dumbing anything down.

people.name: James Chappell
known_for: The Potter's Complete Book of Clay and Glazes
people.notes: Wrote the single most comprehensive recipe reference book in studio ceramics — hundreds of recipes with chemistry, organized by type, cone, and atmosphere. A workhorse book that sits next to the kiln, not on the shelf. Multiple revised editions.

people.name: Greg Daly
people.notes: Leading authority on luster glazes and their chemistry. Covers a domain nobody else in glaze literature treats with this depth — the metallic oxide reduction films that create iridescent surfaces. Also expert in fuming techniques.

people.name: Gabriel Kline
known_for: Crystalline Glazes: Understanding the Process and Materials
people.notes: Wrote the modern bible of crystalline glaze chemistry. Covers zinc silicate crystal nucleation and growth, firing schedules, cooling curves, and seeding techniques with real science. Fills a gap no other single book covers at this depth.

people.name: Phil Rogers
people.notes: Different from Tichane's historical reconstruction approach — Phil's Ash Glazes is practical wood-ash chemistry focused on studio use. Also wrote on salt glazes. Welsh potter who worked with local materials and understood their chemistry intimately.

people.name: Dave Finkelnburg
people.notes: Active educator who writes extensively about glaze fit, thermal expansion, crazing vs. shivering. Applies analytical chemistry to everyday studio problems. Very active in the online glaze chemistry community — Clayart, Glazy forums, Ceramic Arts Network.

people.name: Hein Severijns
people.notes: Created GlazeMaster, one of the earliest glaze calculation software packages alongside Hansen's Insight. Part of the software-makes-chemistry-accessible story of the 1990s-2000s.

people.name: Steve Branfman
people.notes: Wrote the definitive practical guide to raku chemistry — carbon trapping, metallic lusters, copper reduction, post-firing chemistry. Narrow domain but deep expertise. Runs The Potters Shop in Massachusetts.

people.name: Abu'l-Qasim
known_for: First known treatise on ceramic technology (1301)
people.notes: Wrote the first known treatise on ceramic technology in 1301, documenting the glaze and tile-making traditions of Kashan, Persia. Describes luster glaze preparation, tin opacification for white grounds, frit chemistry, and kiln construction. Everything in glaze chemistry descends from someone deciding to write it down instead of just doing it. This is that person.

people.name: Père d'Entrecolles (François Xavier d'Entrecolles)
known_for: first Western documentation of Chinese porcelain manufacture
people.notes: Jesuit missionary stationed in Jingdezhen, China's porcelain capital. His two letters (1712, 1722) describing Chinese porcelain and glaze manufacture in precise detail were industrial espionage that changed Western ceramics forever. Identified kaolin and petuntse (china stone) as the two essential ingredients. Without him, European high-fire glaze chemistry would have been delayed by decades. The bridge between East and West.

people.name: Josiah Wedgwood
people.notes: Invented systematic experimental method for ceramics a century before anyone else. His Experiment Book documents 5,000+ methodical trials — varying one ingredient at a time, recording results. Invented a pyrometer for kiln temperature measurement. Was doing what Ian Currie formalized 200 years later. Fellow of the Royal Society. If Seger is the father of glaze calculation, Wedgwood is the father of glaze experimentation.

people.name: Gail Nichols
known_for: soda vs salt glaze chemistry distinction
people.notes: Wrote the definitive book on soda glazing chemistry. Covers the vapor deposition mechanism, how sodium carbonate interacts with different clay bodies, and the surface chemistry that distinguishes soda from salt firing. Fills a major domain gap — soda firing is chemically distinct from salt (sodium carbonate vs sodium chloride) but often lumped together.

people.name: Mimi Obstler
known_for: Out of the Earth, Into the Fire
ceramic materials science for studio potters
people.notes: Bridges materials science and studio practice in a way that's genuinely accessible. Out of the Earth, Into the Fire covers thermal behavior, mineralogy, phase changes, and chemical reactions in the kiln — everything Parmelee and Norton cover but written for people who fire kilns, not run factories. Often the first recommendation when a potter asks 'I want to understand WHY glazes do what they do.'

people.name: Henrik Norsker
people.notes: Co-authored Glazes for the Self-Reliant Potter (with James Danisch) — how to develop glazes from local rocks, ash, and clay when commercial materials aren't available. Published through a developing-world ceramics organization. Represents a fundamentally different approach: geology-first instead of catalog-first. This is the original approach to glaze chemistry — every tradition before suppliers existed worked this way.

people.name: Clary Illian
people.notes: Studied under Warren MacKenzie, who studied under Bernard Leach. Represents the third generation of the Leach tradition with real chemistry understanding, not just aesthetics. A Potter's Workbook is the most practical 'here's how to think about glazes from raw materials' text in that lineage. Completes the Leach → MacKenzie → Illian chain.

people.name: Pieter Mostert
people.notes: Active researcher doing computational analysis of the Glazy dataset. His glazy-data-analysis GitHub repository is prior art for statistical analysis of glaze chemistry space — clustering, visualization, and pattern discovery in oxide data. Python-based, open-source. Represents the emerging intersection of data science and ceramic chemistry.

people.name: Masakazu Kusakabe
people.notes: Co-authored Japanese Wood-Fired Ceramics (with Marc Lancet) — scientific treatment of anagama and noborigama atmosphere chemistry, ash deposit mechanisms, wadding chemistry. Not romanticized — actual kiln science from a Japanese master who bridges Japanese empirical tradition and Western analytical frameworks. Fills the gap for Japanese wood-firing science from the inside.

people.name: Felix Singer
known_for: ceramic materials science at engineering depth
comprehensive treatment of ceramic processing, glazes, and firing
people.notes: Co-authored with his son W.L. Singer. The most comprehensive engineering-level treatment of ceramics published in English in the 20th century — 1,340 pages covering raw materials, glaze chemistry, kiln design, firing, and testing. The reference engineers reach for when other books don't go deep enough.

people.name: William M. Carty
known_for: Validation of optimal Stull chart zone using modern analytical methods (2012, with Quinlan)
people.notes: Alfred University ceramic engineering professor whose 2012 work with Frederica Quinlan applied modern analytical methods to validate the optimal glaze chemistry zone first identified by Stull in 1912. Their findings confirmed the matte/gloss boundary and durable zone positions on the SiO₂–Al₂O₃ chart, establishing the analytical basis for the optimal 0.3 R²O:0.7 RO zone.

books.title: Industrial Ceramics
subject: ceramic engineering
raw materials
glaze chemistry
kiln design
firing
ceramic testing
books.notes: Co-authored with W.L. Singer. The most comprehensive engineering-level ceramics text published in English in the 20th century — 1,340 pages. Covers raw materials, glaze chemistry, kiln design, firing curves, and physical testing with a depth that no studio-oriented book approaches. Engineers and technical ceramicists reach for Singer & Singer when everything else is insufficient. Foundational reference for ceramic science programs worldwide.

books.title: Glaze Melt Chemistry and the Stull Chart: Validating the Optimal Zone
subject: Stull Chart
melt chemistry
SiO2-Al2O3 field
glaze zone validation
durability
books.notes: Carty and Quinlan applied modern analytical methods to re-examine the SiO₂–Al₂O₃ field first mapped by Stull (1912), confirming and refining the positions of key zone boundaries. Their work established the analytical basis for the 'optimal zone' (roughly 0.3 R²O:0.7 RO, 7:1 SiO₂:Al₂O₃). Cited as 'Carty/Quinlan 2012' in subsequent literature.

books.title: Influences of Variable Silica and Alumina on Porcelain Glazes of Constant RO
books.venue: Transactions of the American Ceramic Society
subject: glaze surface quality
silica-alumina ratio
UMF
glaze classification
matte vs. gloss
books.notes: THE landmark paper. Stull systematically fired hundreds of glaze compositions at cone 11, holding the RO (flux) column constant while varying SiO₂ and Al₂O₃. The resulting chart — glaze surface quality mapped against SiO₂ vs. Al₂O₃ — became the Stull Chart. Defined the navigable regions (matte, semi-matte, bright gloss, underfired, crazed) still used today. Public domain — scanned and indexed on Archive.org.

books.title: Deformation Temperatures of Some Porcelain Glazes
books.venue: Transactions of the American Ceramic Society
subject: deformation temperature
cone 9
silica-alumina ratio
glaze surface
firing temperature dependence
books.notes: Stull and Howat extend the 1912 work to cone 9, mapping deformation temperatures across the SiO₂–Al₂O₃ field. Identifies three key lines on the chart: AB (best gloss), CD (minimum deformation vs. silica), EF (minimum deformation vs. alumina). Proves that optimal glaze regions shift with firing temperature — the zones on the Stull chart are not fixed but temperature-dependent. Public domain — scanned on Archive.org.

books.title: Clay and Glazes for the Potter
subject: materials
firing behavior
glaze chemistry
books.notes: Standard text for generations. Described as having a voice — unhurried, curious, authoritative. Treasured the way musicians treasure a particular record.

knowledge_indexed.label: Full text indexed (237 pages OCR'd from public domain edition)

books.title: A Potter's Book
subject: studio pottery
ash glazes
feldspathic glazes
philosophy of making
books.notes: Contains glaze chemistry embedded in a philosophy of making. Treasured in a devotional way beyond technical utility. Proto-terroir glaze work.

books.title: The Potter's Dictionary of Materials and Techniques
subject: materials
techniques
chemistry
processes
books.notes: Arguably the single most comprehensive reference in the field. Treasured like a trusted tool — worn spines, penciled annotations, tabs.

books.title: The Complete Guide to High-Fire Glazes
subject: high-fire glazes
glaze recipes
glaze chemistry
books.notes: Most-used studio reference of last two decades. Recipe-heavy but with solid chemistry explanations.

books.title: The Complete Guide to Mid-Range Glazes
subject: mid-range glazes
cone 4-7
glaze chemistry

books.title: The Ceramic Spectrum
subject: colorants
color development
oxide chemistry
books.notes: Organized around the periodic table of colorants. People describe it as the book that made color chemistry finally make sense.

books.title: Stoneware Glazes: A Systematic Approach
subject: glaze testing
grid blend methodology
systematic testing
books.notes: Described as transformative — changes how you think rather than just what you know. Before-and-after book for many potters.

books.title: Mastering Cone 6 Glazes
subject: cone 6
functional ware
glaze chemistry
limit formulas
books.notes: Validated middle-temperature functional ware as worthy of rigorous chemistry attention. Treasured by functional ware potters.

books.title: Ceramic Glazes
subject: glaze science
industrial ceramics
firing behavior
thermal expansion
viscosity
books.notes: Dense industrial science heavyweight. Out of print and sought after. Most technically complete treatment of glaze physics and chemistry from a formulation standpoint.

books.title: Ash Glazes
subject: ash glazes
historical reconstruction
books.notes: Self-published, small print runs. Treasured partly because hard to find.

books.title: Chinese Glazes
subject: Chinese ceramic chemistry
historical glaze reconstruction
spectrographic analysis
books.notes: Treasured by a smaller audience but with unusual intensity. Most rigorous scientific treatment of classical Chinese ceramic chemistry in English.

books.title: Electric Kiln Ceramics
subject: oxidation glazes
electric kiln
glaze chemistry
books.notes: Addressed oxidation firing chemistry seriously when most literature assumed reduction.

books.title: Ceramic Science for the Potter
subject: ceramic science
silica-alumina ratios
eutectic behavior
industrial methods
books.notes: Bridges engineering and studio practice more precisely than most books aimed at potters.

books.title: The Potter's Book of Glaze Recipes
subject: glaze recipes
studio glazes

books.title: Wood-Fired Stoneware and Porcelain
subject: wood fire
ash deposition
reduction atmospheres
stoneware
porcelain
books.notes: Covers chemistry of ash deposition and reduction atmospheres with analytical rigor.

books.title: The Complete Potter's Companion
subject: pottery
glazes
general ceramics
books.notes: Reached a mass audience; introduced glaze chemistry to potters who might not have sought it out.

books.title: The Potter's Studio Clay and Glaze Handbook
subject: clay bodies
glazes
materials

books.title: Cushing's Handbook
subject: glaze recipes
glaze chemistry
studio reference
books.notes: Not commercially published; circulated through Alfred University orbit. Treasured almost as artifact. Having a copy feels like connection to a lineage.

books.title: The Potter's Craft
books.notes: One of the first American texts treating pottery making as both craft and science. Includes glaze chemistry fundamentals from a working potter's perspective. Written by the founder of Alfred University's ceramics program.

books.title: Grand Feu Ceramics
books.notes: First serious treatment of high-fire glaze chemistry from a practitioner. Originally in French (Grand Feu Céramiques), translated to English by Samuel Robineau. Covers crystalline glazes, flambés, and high-fire effects with scientific rigor decades ahead of its time.

books.title: Introduction to Ceramics
books.notes: THE ceramic material science textbook for 60+ years. Co-authored with H.K. Bowen and D.R. Uhlmann. Covers phase equilibria, thermal expansion, viscosity, and every physical property relevant to glaze chemistry. Dense, technical, irreplaceable. Revised as 'Introduction to Ceramics' 2nd ed. (1976).

books.title: Understanding Pottery Glazes
books.notes: One of the clearest explanations of glaze chemistry for studio potters ever published. Bridges industrial ceramic science and studio practice without dumbing it down. British perspective.

books.title: The Potter's Complete Book of Clay and Glazes
books.notes: The single most comprehensive recipe reference in studio ceramics. Hundreds of recipes organized by type, cone, and atmosphere, with chemistry. A workhorse book — sits next to the kiln. Multiple revised editions including 2nd ed. (2004).

books.title: Lustre
books.notes: Definitive guide to luster glaze chemistry and technique. Covers metallic oxide reduction films, fuming, and iridescent surfaces. Nobody else covers this domain at this depth.

books.title: Glazes and Glazing Techniques
books.notes: Broad glaze chemistry reference with particular strength in color development and specialty effects from an Australian perspective.

books.title: Crystalline Glazes: Understanding the Process and Materials
books.notes: The modern bible of crystalline glaze chemistry. Covers zinc silicate crystal nucleation and growth, firing schedules, cooling curves, and seeding techniques with real science. The book the crystalline community was waiting for.

books.title: Ash Glazes
books.notes: Practical wood-ash chemistry focused on studio use. Different from Tichane's historical reconstruction approach — this is about making ash glazes work in a modern studio. Covers sourcing, washing, testing, and formulating.

books.title: Mastering Raku
books.notes: Definitive guide to raku chemistry and technique — carbon trapping, metallic lusters, copper reduction, post-firing reduction chemistry. Multiple editions.

books.title: Soda, Clay, and Fire
books.notes: Definitive guide to soda firing chemistry. Covers sodium carbonate vapor deposition mechanisms, clay body interactions, surface chemistry differences from salt firing. The book that finally gave soda firing its own scientific identity separate from salt.

books.title: Out of the Earth, Into the Fire
books.notes: Ceramic materials science written for studio potters. Covers thermal behavior, mineralogy, phase changes, and kiln chemistry. Bridges the gap between dense academic texts (Kingery, Norton) and recipe books. Often the first recommendation for potters wanting to understand the 'why' behind glaze behavior.

books.title: Glazes for the Self-Reliant Potter
books.notes: Co-authored with James Danisch. How to develop glazes from local rocks, ash, and clay without commercial suppliers. Published for developing-world ceramics programs. A fundamentally different approach to glaze chemistry — geology-first, using what the land provides. Also relevant to any potter exploring local-materials practice.

books.title: A Potter's Workbook
books.notes: The most practical materials-based glaze thinking text in the Leach tradition. Third-generation knowledge (Leach → MacKenzie → Illian) distilled into a working guide. Teaches potters to think from materials outward rather than from recipes downward.

books.title: Japanese Wood-Fired Ceramics
books.notes: Co-authored with Marc Lancet. Scientific treatment of Japanese wood-firing traditions — anagama and noborigama kiln atmosphere chemistry, ash deposit mechanisms, wadding chemistry, firing schedules. Bridges Japanese empirical kiln knowledge and Western analytical frameworks.

institutions.name: Alfred University
institutions.significance: Major center for ceramic arts and engineering education; home to Cushing

institutions.name: Digitalfire
institutions.significance: Largest accessible glaze chemistry database and educational resource; Insight software; paradigm shift in computational glaze formulation

concepts.name: Unity Molecular Formula (UMF)
concepts.description: Framework organizing glaze materials into flux, stabilizer (alumina), and glass former (silica) categories for rational glaze calculation

concepts.name: Currie Grid Blend Method
concepts.description: Systematic grid-based approach to glaze testing that maps silica and alumina variations across a matrix of test tiles

concepts.name: Limit Formulas
concepts.description: UMF-based ranges defining safe, functional, and aesthetically predictable glaze formulas for given firing temperatures

concepts.name: Stull Charts
concepts.description: Binary phase diagrams plotting silica against alumina (in UMF) to map glaze behavior — glossy, matte, dry, underfired — across compositional space. A foundational visualization tool for predicting glaze surface from oxide ratios, widely used alongside Currie Grids and limit formulas.

concepts.name: Phase Diagrams & Eutectics
concepts.notes: Phase diagrams map which mineral phases are stable at given temperature and composition. Eutectics are compositions where two or more materials melt together at a temperature lower than either would alone — the fundamental reason glazes melt where they do. The SiO₂-Al₂O₃ binary phase diagram underpins the Stull chart. Understanding eutectics explains why a glaze melts at cone 6 when none of its individual ingredients would.

concepts.name: Thermal Expansion & Glaze Fit
concepts.notes: When a glaze and clay body cool from kiln temperature, they contract at different rates determined by their coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE). If the glaze contracts more than the body, it goes into tension → crazing (fine cracks). If it contracts less, it goes into compression → shivering (glaze flaking). Perfect 'glaze fit' means the glaze is in slight compression. This is the #1 practical problem in studio ceramics and the reason potters adjust silica, alumina, and flux ratios. Calculated from oxide contributions using Appen factors or measured with a dilatometer.

concepts.name: Crystalline Glaze Theory
concepts.notes: Crystalline glazes produce visible crystals (typically zinc silicate — willemite, Zn₂SiO₄) that grow during controlled cooling. The chemistry requires: high zinc oxide (25-30% of flux), high silica, very low alumina (alumina inhibits crystal growth), and a specific cooling schedule — rapid cooling to nucleation temperature (~1050°C), then a long hold (2-8 hours) while crystals grow, then final cooling. Nucleation sites can be seeded. The result is predictable in chemistry but chaotic in pattern — every firing is unique. First achieved systematically by Taxile Doat at Sèvres, then by Robineau in America.

metadata.source: Compiled from conversation covering canonical glaze chemistry figures, popular books, and community-treasured texts
metadata.notes: Kyle Crowder listed in source material but flagged as unverifiable — excluded from this dataset pending verification
